coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen atom free polythiophene derivative as an efficient chemosensor for highly selective and sensitive Cu2+ and Ag+ detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Qian, Junning
Wu, Di
Cai, Ping
Xia, Jiangbin
coredata.dc:description: A new nitrogen atom free polythiophene derivative bearing methoxy-ethoxy units of poly{3-[2-(2-methoxy-ethoxy)-ethoxy]-thiophene} (PM) was successfully synthesized by introducing multiple ether bonds on the thiophene unit. The special (ether bonds) coordination structure was constructed and these fluorescence characteristics of PM to metal ions detection were investigated. This polythiophene-based material displays a specific fluorescence quenching effect on Cu2+ and Ag+, and correspondingly emerges some color changes that are visible to the naked eyes. In addition, it even performs a low detection limit to Cu2+ for only 0.45 μM, which exhibits a higher selective detection to Cu2+ than other reported N-containing chemosenors. These discoveries are helpful to indicate an original aspect for development on nitrogen atom free polythiophene-based fluorescent-sensing materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen atom free
Polythiophene derivative
Coordination structure
Fluorescence characteristics
Cu2+ and Ag+
Detection limit

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structure and optical characteristics of PM (a) FTIR spectra; (b) Fluorescence spectra of PM in DMSO (10 mg/L). (The excitation peak is 560 nm and the emission peak is 620 nm).
UV–vis adsorption properties of PM (10 mg · L−1) treated with different metal ions (2 nM) (a) in DMSO; (b) in DMF.
Relative intensity changes. Insert: color changes in DMSO (top) and DMF (below), and Cu2+ is circled by blue line and Ag+ is circled by red line.
Fluorescence sensing properties of PM (10 mg·L−1) treated with different metal ions (2 nM) (a) in DMSO; (b) in DMF; (c) relative fluorescent intensity changes.
Schematic illustration of coordination structure formed with Cu2+ or Ag+.
(a) Fluorescence spectra changes of PM (3 mL, 10 mg·L−1) treated with Cu2+ (100 μM, 0–10 mM); (b) corresponding linear relation with (0–16 μM).
(a) Fluorescence spectra changes of PM (3 mL, 10 mg·L−1) treated with Ag+ (100 μM, 0–10 mM); (b) corresponding linear relation with (0–140 μM).
Linear equation and Detection Limit (DL) of PM treated with Cu2+ and Ag+.
The schematic diagram of the changes of PM (3 mL, 10 mg·L−1) fluorescence intensities with different time (treated with different concentrations of Cu2+ and Ag+).

The results of (a) Ag XPS. Inset: empty bottle (left), bottle after the reaction of PM + Ag+ (right); (b) Particles size ranges. Inset: the red-laser (600 nm) irradiation of solutions before and after PM + Ag+ reaction.
CPs chemosensors reported for Cu2+ and Ag+ detection.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen atom free polythiophene derivative as an efficient chemosensor for highly selective and sensitive Cu2+ and Ag+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Science, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Organosilicon Compounds & Materials, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Molecular Science, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Organosilicon Compounds & Materials
College of Chemistry and Molecular Science

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Chemistry and Molecular Science, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new nitrogen atom free polythiophene derivative bearing methoxy-ethoxy units of poly{3-[2-(2-methoxy-ethoxy)-ethoxy]-thiophene} (PM) was successfully synthesized by introducing multiple ether bonds on the thiophene unit. The special (ether bonds) coordination structure was constructed and these fluorescence characteristics of PM to metal ions detection were investigated. This polythiophene-based material displays a specific fluorescence quenching effect on Cu2+ and Ag+, and correspondingly emerges some color changes that are visible to the naked eyes. In addition, it even performs a low detection limit to Cu2+ for only 0.45 μM, which exhibits a higher selective detection to Cu2+ than other reported N-containing chemosenors. These discoveries are helpful to indicate an original aspect for development on nitrogen atom free polythiophene-based fluorescent-sensing materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Along with economic development, metal ion pollution is becoming more and more serious through wastewater discharge, especially for these heavy metal ions, such as Cr3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Zn2+ and so on [1,2]. This is because that these metal ions are easily enriched through the food chain, which constitutes a severe threat to the health of human beings, like menke syndrome, anaemia and minamata disease, etc. [3,4] Therefore, it is of great significance to effectively monitor metal ions concentration in wastewater for the implementation of environmental protection policies.

Traditional detection methods are usually based on experimental apparatuses, such as X-Ray Fluorescence (XRF), X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), etc. [5,6] However, these measurement machines require a lot of instruments and equipments, which are expensive and inconvenient for wide application. Based on the current researches of metal ions detection, it is urgent to develop simple, fast-response and cheap method to detect metal ions. As a new sensing means, chemosensors are more and more active in the field of detection because of their low price [7,8]. Rapid development in exploiting and designing novel chemosensors for detecting metal ions has been made over these years. Nowadays, lots of materials have been successfully applied as chemosensors. For instance, Wang et al. developed a new kind of CdTe QDs for metal cations detection, which was a highly sensitive and selective chemosensor, showing a promising application in the detection field [9]. Lv and his coauthors successfully prepared an enhanced MOF-based material chemosensor in detecting metal ions, and this brilliant optical performance enabled it to be one of these candidates of metal ions sensors [10]. Besides, Hai et al. built a nanoscale Ag particles sensor doping on wood for metal ions sensing for the first time, and this material showed a lower detection limit of 32 pM for detecting Hg2+. This result was even lower than the detecting standards in water [11]. Through the study of rotaxane, Denis et al. found that this sort of interlocked molecules could be well applied to the design of the molecular structure, thus expressing remarkable sensitive fluorescence quenching to metal ions [12]. Furthermore, Wu and coauthors prepared a new fluorescent chemosensor CMCA, and it was highly sensitive to existence of N2H4 or HSO3 −. The detection of CMCA for HSO3 − could even reach 20 nM, which also showed a wide application in the living cells [13]. Sakunkaewkasem et al. prepared a new chemosensor based on [5] helicene derivative. This probe could highly recognize Cu2+ and Zn2+ in aqueous system, and detection of this sensor for Cu2+ and Zn2+ were calculated as 5.6 and 3.8 ppb, respectively [14]. Through the analysis of intramolecular atomic sites, Huang et al. firstly synthesized several bisbenzimidazole-based chemosensors. The three sensors were very sensitive and selective to Fe3+ or Ag+ under different pH, which were also stable in the environment [15]. Wu's group constructed one “on-off” model NBS-CDs chemosensor, and successfully applied it to the detection of L-Cys and Ag+ in cells. This simple and high standard material would greatly expand its application in other detection fields [16]. With the enhancement of people awareness of environmental protection, the requirements for metal ions detection has become higher than past [17]. Thus, it is crucial to develop novel chemosensors with excellent performance in this field.

Recently, the investigation and application of conjugated polymers (CPs) have developed rapidly [18,19]. This is a kind of macromolecule composed of conjugated groups with π-electrons. Because of the transition of π − π* electrons between polymer chains, CPs exhibit an excellent light capture ability [20]. This characteristic enables them to be widely studied in electro-optic field [21,22]. Among different CPs, polythiophene and its derivatives have attracted broad attention due to their low price and high thermal stability and excellent electroconductivity [23]. All these prominent features enable polythiophene-based polymers to be ideal materials in the electro-optic field, such as electroluminescent devices and solar cells, etc. [24,25] Nevertheless, in addition to mainstream field of electrochemical, there is also a small number of polythiophene-based polymers applications in sensing area. Xing et al. synthesized a polymer with the base of thiophene and the thiophene-based material showed an excellent selectivity for Cu2+. This special detection to metal ions has greatly expanded the application of polythiophene derivatives [26]. Guo and co-workers have successfully achieved a new polythiophene derivative containing N/O ligand, and they further applied it in the detection for Cu2+ and amino acids. The detecting level for Cu2+ of this material was far below the international standard. The results showed that these polythiophene derivatives had great potential application in sensing field [27]. Yao and co-workers have successfully prepared a supramolecular complex based on polythiophene derivative, and they applied it to Cu2+ detection in aqueous system. It was based on a thiophene structure, and the detecting level for Cu2+ of this material was 0.05 mM. The results showed that these polythiophene derivatives had great potential application in sensing field [28]. In addition, Yao et al. synthesized the poly(2-(4-methyl-3-thienyloxy)propanesulfonate and they applied it as the Cu2+ sensor, which surprisingly exhibited a detection limit of 3 μM [29]. However, to the best of our knowledge, most of these CPs chemosensors applied to the metal ions detecting field are basically introduced with N atom because of its particular coordination properties with metal ions [27,30]. Few reports are focused on crown-ether-polythiophene derivatives applied in metal ions detection. This is due to the difficulty in introducing polyether bond groups and further polymerization through oxidation or metal catalysis.

In this work, on account of current circumstances of the researches for polythiophene derivatives in sensing area, a novel poly{3-[2-(2-methoxy-ethoxy)-ethoxy]-thiophene} (PM) is successfully synthesized and applied in the field of metal ions detection. Moreover, this material presents excellent fluorescent sensing properties to Cu2+ and Ag+ when they are in a tiny concentration range, and the detection limit of it for Cu2+ even reaches only 0.45 μM. We believe that it will largely broaden the application of these polymers in sensing field.

The first-step product (3-[2-(2-methoxy-ethoxy)-ethoxy]-thiophene) was synthesized according to the following procedures [31]. 40 mL degme and 2.3 g Na were added into a flask under 60 °C. Then put 0.955 g CuI, 5 mL DMF and 16.1 g 3-bromothiophene into the mixture for 12 h under 135 °C. Eventually, the product (3-[2-(2-methoxy-ethoxy)-ethoxy]-thiophene) was prepared and separated by chromatography and other ways. Later, (3-[2-(2-methoxy-ethoxy)-ethoxy]-thiophene), 4.41 g NBS and 50 mL DMF were treated with continuously stirring for about 5 h. Then a series of methods were employed to separate out the monomer (2-bromo-3-[2-(2-methoxy-ethoxy)-ethoxy]-thiophene) which was named as MB. This monomer was later characterized with 13C NMR and 1H NMR (Figs. S1 and S2). 1H NMR (100 MH, CDCl3) δ 7.21 (s, 1H), 6.79 (s, 1H), 4.23 (s, 2H), 3.85 (s, 2H), 3.75 (s, 2H), 3.59 (s, 2H), 3.41 (s, 3H); 13C NMR (100 MH, CDCl3) δ 154.27, 124.30, 117.99, 92.29, 71.94, 71.66, 70.86, 69.82, 59.20.

The obtained monomer 2-bromo-3-[2-(2-methoxy-ethoxy)-ethoxy]-thiophene was subsequently mixed with 10 mL toluene and 0.5 mL boron trifluoride diethyl etherate (BFEE), keeping stirring for 24 h. Finally, the generated product was deeply purified and separated. So as to facilitate the polymer conveniently, the powder sample was named as PM. The whole preparation way of PM was depicted as Scheme 1 .

13C NMR and 1H NMR data were gained from Mercury VX-300 MHz NMR machine. Mass spectrum information were collected on ESI-MS spectrometer. Bruker Equinox-55 was employed to test the types of chemical bonds in monomer and polymer structures. Related optical characteristics were obtained from UV–vis-NIR (UV-3600), which is operated at room temperature from 300 to 1000 nm. In addition, the fluorescence spectra were measured by Shimadzu RF-5301PC excited by xenon lamp light source. Related molecular weight data of PM were collected from GPC (PL-GPC 220), and DMF was used as the solvent for polymer. The Thermo Fisher Escalab 250Xi was used for the measurement of silver species chemical states after the reaction between AgNO3 and polythiophene-based polymer, applying Al Kα as the exciting source. The nano particles formed after the reaction between AgNO3 and polymer were characterized on DLS (Malvern Zetasizer machine). Moreover, AR reagents (NaCl, MgCl2, AlCl3, SnCl4, CrCl3, MnCl2, AgNO3, CoCl2, Ni(NO3)2, ZnCl2, CdCl2, CuCl2, FeCl3) were employed for further metal ions selective detection in deionized H2O of PM solution.

Fluorescent spectra were determined by adding different concentration (0.1 mM, 0.2 mM, 0.3 mM, 0.4 mM, 0.5 mM, 1 mM, 2 mM, 3 mM, 4 mM, 5 mM, 10 mM) of Cu2+ (100 μM/H2O) into PM solution (3 mL, 10 mg/L).
Fluorescent spectra were determined by adding different concentration (0.2 mM, 0.4 mM, 0.8 mM, 2 mM, 4 mM, 6 mM, 8 mM, 10 mM) of Cu2+ (100 μM/H2O) into PM solution (3 mL, 10 mg/L).

The structural and optical characteristics of PM were characterized by FT-IR and Fluorometric spectra. As seen from Fig. 1(a), a large peak range from 2800 cm−1 to 3000 cm−1 is assigned to stretching of aliphatic hydrocarbon [32]. Besides, CC and CC bands on thiophene ring appear in these positions (1636 cm−1, 1511 cm−1, 1337 cm−1) [33]. Furthermore, one peak at 1078 cm−1 is relevant to the band of COC stretching in the sample [34]. Another characteristic peak at around 695 cm−1 is associated with the C-S-C band [34]. Especially, a peak observed at 792 cm−1 is attributed to the disubstituted thiophenes, which is a signal of coupling of thiophene groups [32]. All these results reveal that the desired PM had been obtained.

Except for the structural characteristic of FT-IR, fluorescence properties were also gained to further investigate material optical performance. As seen from Figs. 1(b) and S3, the fluorescence spectra of PM are highly similar in DMSO and DMF. They emerge an emission peak (620 nm) based on the excitation of 560 nm. And the Stokes shift of PM is about 60 nm, which is caused by the fluorescence formed in the movement of exciton to unrestricted units [35,36]. Moreover, an average molecular weight of PM is measured as 3.0 KDa with the degree of polymerization of 15.

Enlightened by special optical performances of polythiophene, we have studied the sensing characteristics of PM with different metal ions. As seen from Fig. 2(a) and (b), PM exhibits a broadly absorptive peak at around 523 nm in DMF and 527 nm in DMSO, respectively. Moreover, the absorption boundary of PM reaches 673 nm. And the corresponding optical band gap is calculated as 1.84 eV, which is lower than that of P3HT, [37] revealing a promising application in optics field. To evaluate the sensing properties of PM, UV–vis adsorption of PM were carried with 13 metal ions (Fig. 2(a)). PM almost shows no obvious UV–vis adsorption changes in DMSO after adding different metal ions (Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Sn4+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cd2+), and they all emerge a similar adsorption peak at around 523 nm. However, different peaks appear at 505 nm (Fe3+, Cu2+) and 457 nm (Ag+), implying some metal ions dependence. These blue-shift results reveal that Fe3+, Cu2+ and Ag+ can form more stable complexes with PM, which is attributed to the stronger coordination ability of these three metal ions. Furthermore, both Fe3+ and Cu2+ present a longer wavelength than Ag+. This result implies that adding Fe3+ and Cu2+ can make it easier for the transition of electron from PM that adding Ag+ [38].

On the other hand, the UV–vis adsorption of PM with different metal ions in DMF (Fig. 2(b)) are nearly the same as those in DMSO. These metal ions (Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Sn4+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cd2+) still do not react with PM, but showing a peak at 527 nm. Besides, Cu2+(509 nm) and Ag+ (498 nm) appear to be more reactive than other ions. However, unlike in DMSO, Fe3+ seems to weakly interact with PM in DMF, and the position and intensity of adsorption peak are nearly unchanged. Compared with absorption peaks in DMF, all absorption peaks in DMSO solution appear to be blue-shifted, which is attributed to greater polarity of DMSO [39]. In addition, the relative intensity changes results of UV–vis adsorption are shown in Fig. 3 . Under equal testing environment and ion concentration (2 × 10−3 mol/L), PM expresses better selectivity to a few ions (Ag+ and Cu2+) in both DMSO and DMF than other ions, including Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Sn4+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cd2+. However, Fe3+ displays different conditions as it emerges a weakened absorption intensity in DMSO but no change of absorption intensity in DMF. Interestingly, the absorption intensity of PM gets decreased after adding Cu2+, while the absorption intensity gets enhanced after the addition of Ag+. The decreased adsorption intensity is due to the strong coordination ability of Cu2+ with PM, forming a stable coordination structure (see in Fluorescence Sensing Characteristics section). Nevertheless, the enhancement of absorption intensity for Ag+ would be attributed to the generation of Ag particles. [40]

Moreover, as shown in Fig. 3, the color of PM was changed by the addition of these metal ions. It can be seen that PM presents no obvious color changes after adding Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Sn4+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cd2+ in DMSO and DMF, respectively. But the addition of Ag+ and Cu2+ makes the PM color change to brown. However, the addition of Cu2+ makes the solution color become colorless in DMF. These results reveal that PM material is promising in the field by naked eyes for Cu2+ and Ag+ detection.

As shown in Fig. 4 , based on addition of common cations (Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Sn4+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Ag+) in PM (10 mg/L) in DMSO and DMF, it is clear from Fig. 4(a) that Cu2+ and Ag+ have apparent fluorescence quenching to PM (see in Fig. S4), and Fe3+ exhibits a weak fluorescence quenching to PM, while the addition other cations do not cause a change in fluorescence. Therefore, it can be inferred that PM has strong selectivity to Cu2+ and Ag+ in DMSO according to the fluorescence changes above, and it displays a weak sensitivity to the existence of Fe3+ in DMSO. Moreover, the finding of decreased fluorescence shows consistency with UV–vis adsorptions except adding Ag+. Then we also investigate the fluorescence sensing properties of PM with metal ions in DMF. As shown in Fig. 4(b), the fluorescence characteristics of PM are similar in DMF and DMSO. It almost shows no apparent change with that in DMSO. However, fluorescence intensity of PM appears to be weakly decreased after being engaged with Fe3+, which is not obvious in DMF. This result reveals that different solvents have certain influence on fluorescence characteristics [41].

The emission intensities of PM in DMF (IDMF) and DMSO (IDMSO) are employed as a reference, and relative intensities of PM treated with different metal ions (Nomalized Intensity = I/IDMF/DMSO) in these two solvents are shown in Fig. 4(c). These changes obviously manifest that the emission intensity of PM has little or no change expect adding Ag+ or Cu2+. It is notable that larger change range of PM fluorescence intensity belongs to the addition of Ag+ or Cu2+ whether it is in DMF or DMSO, and the fluorescence quenching shows the best effect in DMF. These results demonstrate that PM performs satisfactory selectivity for detecting Ag+ and Cu2+. As depicted in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence quenching effect is due to the coordination reaction of O atom and S atom with Cu2+ or Ag+, forming a stable ring structure [42–44].

Next, taking into account the presence of remarkable fluorescence quenching of PM after adding Ag+ or Cu2+, the detection results of PM are analyzed by the changes of fluorescence signals collected from the distinct added quantities of Ag+ and Cu2+.

It is notable from Fig. 6(a) that the fluorescence response of PM firstly decreases slowly (Cu2+: 0–0.5 mM) with continuous increase of Cu2+ concentration, followed by a rapid descent process (Cu2+: 0.5–1 mM), eventually it slows down to close to the same level. And it also emerges a similar process when treated with Ag+ (Fig. 7(a)). Furthermore, it can be observed that the position of fluorescence peak gradually moves to the low wavelength when Cu2+ or Ag+ is added in PM. These interesting fluorescence quenching and blue-shifted peaks phenomenon are attributed to the formation of a stably annular coordination structure described in Fig. 8 . Besides, the decreased degree of fluorescence intensity of Cu2+ addition is much greater than that of Ag+ addition (ions content: 100μΜ; 10−2 mol/L), which implies that Cu2+ shows a better fluorescence response to PM than Ag+. Simultaneously, it can be found that there is a properly linear relationship among the maximum fluorescence intensity with different additive amounts of Cu2+ and Ag+, and Stern-Volmer Eq. (1) is usually employed to represent this linear fluorescence quenching effect [45].
(1)
y =
K sv
x
+ 1
y: The ratios of I0 (initial fluorescence intensity) and I (fluorescence intensity with the addition of metal ions); Ksv: Fluorescence quenching coefficient; x: The concentration of quencher.

Especially, PM displays a decreased fluorescence intensity with increased quantity of Cu2+ (Fig. 6(b)), appearing the linear correlation in the range of 0–16 μM (Fig. 6(b)). Meanwhile Ag+ shows the linear correlation in higher range of 0–140 μM (Fig. 7(b)). In addition, fluorescence quenching coefficients Ksv (Cu2+) and Ksv (Ag+) are 3.944 × 103 μM−1 and 1.860 × 103 μM−1 (Fig. 8), respectively, which stands for the sensitivity of metal ions to fluorescence quenching. From the perspective of interaction, different binding capacities of PM to Cu2+ and Ag+ keep the order: Cu2+ > Ag+. Otherwise, the calculated detection limits (DL) for Cu2+ and Ag+ are 0.45 μM and 0.95 μM (Fig. 8), respectively. This lower DL of Cu2+ is mainly because that Cu2+ possesses an empty d orbit and a smaller radius (0.073 nm). Hence, electrons in PM may readily moves to Cu2+ when Cu2+ interacts with PM, resulting in better fluorescence quenching effect. Furthermore, the sensing capacities of PM after adding Ag+ or Cu2+ were also investigated under different temperatures and pH (Figs. S5–S8). It reveals that high temperature is beneficial to reduce the detection limit of PM for Cu2+ and Ag+, while the increased pH or decreased pH can increase the detection limit of PM for Cu2+ and Ag+.

These sensitive characteristics of PM to Cu2+ and Ag+ are same as that of the expanded fluorescence property, which is ascribed to the energy migration towards polymers skeleton during the electron excitation [46]. As shown in Table 1 , this new chemosensor PM exhibits a lower detection limit for Cu2+ recognition than those chemosensors containing N atom. Besides, the detection limit of PM for Ag+ detection is quite low or comparable to those N-containing Ag+ chemosensor. Therefore, PM can be used as a potential fluorescence chemosensor for Cu2+ and Ag+ detection.

To further explore the fluorescence sensing mechanism of Cu2+ and Ag+ to PM, fluorescence detection experiments were constructed at different time. It can be observed in Fig. 9 that fluorescence intensity firstly decreases and then slowly reaches a stable value with the increasing time when Cu2+ or Ag+ is added into PM. As for different concentrations of Cu2+, the fluorescence responses all rapidly decrease to the lowest level within 1 min, which is attributed to the intense complexation with O and S atoms in polymer [42]. Whereas, fluorescence intensity exhibits a trend of gradually decreasing to constant in the existence of Ag+ and the rate of fluorescence quenching incrementally accelerates with the increasing Ag+ concentration. Besides, the fluorescence quenching time becomes longer as the Ag+ concentration increases. These results imply that there are coordination reaction and redox reaction between Ag+ and polymer, and the competition of the coordination reaction and redox reaction leads to longer quenching time [54]. In addition, after 30 min treatment with Cu2+ and Ag+, the fluorescence quenching percentages are {(Cu2+: 2.5%) and (Ag+: 2.0%), 0.1 mM}, {(Cu2+: 37.5%) and (Ag+: 12.0%), 1 mM} and {(Cu2+: 68.0%) and (Ag+: 32.0%), 10 mM}, respectively. These investigations present that PM material is promising and effective as Cu2+ and Ag+ fluorescence detection.

According to these characterizations results above, the reaction processes that account for the formation of Ag0 species are described as Eqs. (2) and (3). This is because that polythiophene and its derivatives can be oxidized by doping of anion to form p-type polymers [55]. It can be seen that there is a redox balance between Ag+ and PM, resulting in the reduction of Ag+ to Ag0.
(2)
Ag solution +
⇔
Ag polymer +
(3)
Polymer ⋅
NO 3
‐
⋅
Ag Polymer +
⇔
Polymer +
⋅
NO 3
‐
⋅
Ag 0

So as to confirm the redox reaction between the Ag+ and PM, XPS and DLS characterizations are carried out upon the reacted products. The fitted Ag3d5/2 XPS spectrum shown in Fig. 10(a) reveals that there are two distinguished types of silver species, which are ascribed to Ag+ (367.7 eV) and Ag0 (368.2 eV), accordingly. In addition, it can be also seen the insert picture in Fig. 10(a) that the inner surface of glass bottle is covered with a black substance after the reaction of AgNO3 and PM for 18 h. This finding and XPS result reflect that Ag0 species are formed after the reaction. In addition, red-laser (600 nm) irradiation is conducted for the solutions before and after reaction, and a bright channel can be clearly observed in the AgNO3 + PM system (Fig. 10(b)). This finding means that the formed particles size are less than the visible light wavelength (400 nm–700 nm) [56]. To further confirm the particles sizes, the DLS measurement of these particles generated from the reaction was then carried out. As seen from Fig. 10(b), a high distribution and a weak distribution can be observed at 122 nm and 142 nm in the range of 0.1 nm–10,000 nm. This result further implies that the size of Ag0 particles formed in reaction are mainly around 122 nm, which agrees with the above Tyndall phenomenon.

In brief, we have synthesized a fluorescence chemosensor based on nitrogen atom free polythiophene derivatives poly{3-[2-(2-methoxy-ethoxy)-ethoxy]-thiophene}, which is much easier on synthesis than N-containing polythiophene derivatives. This polythiophene-based polymer containing methoxy-ethoxy units can interact well with Cu2+ and Ag+ through the coordination characteristics, generating specific fluorescence changes and visible color changes to naked eyes. Furthermore, it performs low DL for Cu2+ and Ag+, which are only 0.45 μM and 0.95 μM, respectively. This article is mianly focused on the aspect of developing one novel method about designing fluorescence chemosensor in view of the coordination effect between polythiophene derivatives and metal ions. We believe that this N-free polythiophene derivative bearing methoxy-ethoxy with excellent fluorescence property would be promising in the field of on environmental detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the Natural Science Foundation of China (21875173), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2042015kf0180) of China and Large-scale Instrument And Equipment Sharing Foundation of Wuhan University for supporting this research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.03.093.
